content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 5, 2016

Josh Fryday
Councilman
City of Novato
38 Arnold Drive
Novato, CA 94949

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. 15-244

Dear Councilmember Fryday:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide therefore assumes that the facts you provided to us are complete and accurate.

     Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

     Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you, a Novato City Councilmember, from participating in decisions about a proposed recreational sports complex approximately 1,650 feet from your residence?

CONCLUSION

     No. While the proposed sports complex could foreseeably affect your financial interest in your residence, any effect would probably be immaterial.

FACTS

     You are a City Councilmember in the City of Novato. The City Council will soon consider whether to approve the proposed Hamilton Fields development, a 55-acre commercial sports and

_______________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-244
Page No. 2

recreation complex and 12-acre neighborhood park in Novato's Hamilton neighborhood. The
project would consist of:

• Five baseball/softball fields, including a 1,000 seat ballpark;
• Two multi-sport playing fields;
• A 50,000 square foot training center;
• A 12-acre community park, including youth baseball field and two multi-sport fields;
and
• Additional support facilities, including concessions and trail systems.

The proposed development site is a large, open field several hundred yards northeast of the
Novato Skatepark. The property currently abuts San Pablo Bay National Wildlife Refuge to the
northwest, Hamilton Parkway to the southwest, Aberdeen Road to the east, and Nave Drive to the
west. You own and live in a single-family residence on Arnold Drive, approximately 0.31 miles
(1,650 feet) southeast of the proposed development as the crow flies, and around 0.7 miles from the
site by car. The area around your home—one of around 2,000 residences built since 1999—is built
out and mostly residential. The primary routes in and out of your neighborhood are Hamilton
Parkway and Main Gate Road/Palm Drive.

Hamilton is the site of a former military base that the City of Novato and private developers
have since repurposed as a "planned community with residential, commercial, open space and civic
uses."2 The proposed development site is located atop the former base landfill, between North
Hamilton Parkway and the Hamilton wetlands restoration project. The former landfill area has been
planned for community recreational development since master planning for the military base
conversion. The landfill area itself belongs to the U.S. Army, which would lease the property to
private developers; city-owned land adjacent to the landfill would be used for parking and
neighborhood park facilities.

The primary access route to Hamilton Fields will be a new access road at or near the
southerly driveway of the Novato Skate Park, near the intersection of North Hamilton Parkway, a
major thoroughfare that feeds into Nave Drive, one of Novato's main traffic arterials. A
preliminary traffic impact analysis of ten intersections near the project site, including North
Hamilton Parkway and Nave Drive, found that most of the affected intersections would continue
operating at acceptable levels of service (LOS) at upon construction of Hamilton Fields. Under
typical conditions, the project is estimated to generate 230 weekday evening peak hour trips and
234 weekend midday peak hour trips; during maximum activity periods like events at the 1,000-seat
ballpark, the project is estimated to generate approximately 448 trips during the evening peak hour
and 626 trips during the midday peak hour.

The preliminary traffic analysis predicts that the unsignalized westbound left turn at Nave
Drive/Roblar Drive may drop to unacceptable LOS E when activity at Hamilton Fields is at a
maximum. The report suggests mitigating this congestion by having the Novato Police Department
implement manual traffic control at the Nave/Roblar intersection during major events. The study
also notes that traffic already periodically exceeds acceptable levels at the signalized intersection of

2 City of Novato, Hamilton Field, http://novato.org/community/get-to-know-novato/hamilton-field (last visited
January 25, 2016).

File No. A-15-244
Page No. 3

Nave Drive and North Hamilton Drive, an issue which may persist or be exacerbated by Hamilton
Fields. The study recommends adding a left turn lane to this intersection, to accommodate
increased traffic flows from Hamilton Fields.

The City of Novato will consider Hamilton Fields' other potential impacts, such as noise
and environmental factors, in an upcoming environmental impact report (EIR).

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of the official's interests. (Section 87103.)

Your real property interest in your residence is a financial interest that could give rise to a
conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act, since it is a direct interest in real property of
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) Therefore, to determine whether you have a conflict of interest
under the Act with respect to the City Council's decisions about the Hamilton Fields proposal, we
must determine whether approval or denial of the project would have a foreseeable, material effect
on your real property interest in your home.

Foreseeability:

To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of governmental decision on a
public official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) Before
deciding whether an effect on your interests would be material, we must first determine whether
any effects are foreseeable.

Regulation 18701 provides two different standards for determining foreseeability,
depending on whether an official's interest is explicitly involved in the decision. In this case, you
are not named party in the Hamilton Fields proposal, nor would its approval involve you
contracting with the City of Novato or receiving a permit or some other entitlement. Your real
property interest in your residence is therefore not explicitly involved in the City Council's
decisions about Hamilton Fields. (Regulation 18701(a).) Where an official's interest is not
explicitly involved in a governmental decision, we have found that the effect of the decision "[…]
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable," but rather, the project's effect on the
official's finances must merely be "[…] a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical […]." (Regulation 18701(b), emphasis added.)

Materiality:

Since it is foreseeable that the City Council's approval or denial of Hamilton Fields could
have a financial effect on your real property interest in your residence, we must next determine
whether that effect would be material.

Regulation 18702.2(a) lists several circumstances under which a financial effect on an
official's real property interest is material. Based on your facts, the City of Novato's decisions

File No. A-15-244
Page No. 4

about Hamilton Fields could have a material financial effect on your real property under two
circumstances: a change in the character of the parcel, or an influence on its market value.
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(10)(12).)

Change of the parcel's character: We have found that a governmental decision will have a
material financial effect on an official's real property interests if it "[w]ould change the character of the
parcel of real property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10), emphasis added.) To determine whether an
effect is changing the character of a parcel, we must examine four factors: traffic or intensity of use;
view; parking; and privacy, noise and air quality. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10).)

Traffic or Intensity of Use: Your home is located approximately 1,650 feet from the project
site. The areas surrounding your home are built out. The preliminary traffic study indicates that all
major intersections near the project will continue operating at acceptable levels of service after
completion of Hamilton Fields, with the exception of the westbound left turn at Nave Drive/Roblar
Drive and the Nave Drive and North Hamilton Drive intersection. Based on these facts and the
findings in the study, any impacts of additional traffic generated by Hamilton Fields will be either
insignificant or mitigatable and will not affect the use of your property.

Parking: Hamilton Fields will have its own parking area adjacent to the complex. Therefore,
any increase in vehicles parking near Hamilton Fields would not affect your property.

View: Based on the facts you have provided, Hamilton Fields will not be visible from your
property, since you live in a flat, built-out neighborhood.

Privacy, Noise & Air Quality: The City of Novato has not yet completed its EIR on
Hamilton Fields. But the 1,650-foot distance between the proposed site and your residence
suggests that there will be little, if any, effect on the privacy, noise or air quality of your property.

Influence on Market Value: Having established that Hamilton Fields would not change the
character of your parcel, we must examine whether "the governmental decision [is] of such a nature
that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of [your] property."
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(12).) The standard for making this determination is that of "a reasonably
prudent person using due care and consideration under the circumstances."

Under these facts, we find that it is unlikely that the increase in traffic on Hamilton Parkway
would influence the market value of your residence. As described in the traffic impact analysis, any
traffic impacts caused by Hamilton Fields would be could most likely be mitigated by manual
traffic control and by adding a left turn lane at the westbound Nave Drive/Roblar Drive intersection.
The Nave Drive/Roblar intersection is approximately 1.3 miles from your home by car, while the
Nave Drive/North Hamilton Parkway intersection is about 1 mile from your house. Thus, while
Hamilton Fields could have a foreseeable effect on your real property interest in your residence, it is
unlikely that any effect would be material.

For the foregoing reasons, we find that Act does not prohibit you from participating in the
Novato City Council's decisions about Hamilton Fields. (See, e.g. Seto Advice Letter, No. A-15-
177.)

File No. A-15-244
Page No. 5

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-6421.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Toren A. Lewis
       Counsel, Legal Division

TAL:jgl

sections.question:
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you, a Novato City Councilmember, 
from participating in decisions about a proposed recreational sports complex approximately 1,650 
feet from your residence?
sections.conclusion:
No. While the proposed sports complex could foreseeably affect your financial interest in 
your residence, any effect would probably be immaterial.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, 
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the official's interests. (Section 87103.)

conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act, since it is a direct interest in real property of 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) Therefore, to detennine whether you have a conflict of interest 
under the Act with respect to the City Council's decisions about the Hamilton Fields proposal, we 
must determine whether approval or denial of the project would have a foreseeable, material effect 
on your real property interest in your home. 
Foreseeability: 
To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of governmental decision on a 
public official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) Before 
deciding whether an effect on your interests would be material, we must first determine whether 
any effects are foreseeable. 
Regulation 1870 l provides two different standards for determining foreseeability, 
depending on whether an official's interest is explicitly involved in the decision. In this case, you 
are not named party in the Hamilton Fields proposal, nor would its approval involve you 
contracting with the City of Novato or receiving a permit or some other entitlement. Your real

decisions about Hamilton Fields. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Where an official's interest is not 
explicitly involved in a governmental decision, we have found that the effect of the decision"[ ... ] 
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable," but rather, the project's effect on the 
official's finances must merely be"[ ... ] a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical [ ... ]." (Regulation 18701 (b ), emphasis added.)

Since it is foreseeable that the City Council's approval or denial of Hamilton Fields could 
have a financial effect on your real property interest in your residence, we must next determine 
whether that effect would be material. 
Regulation 18702.2(a) lists several circumstances under which a financial effect on an 
official's real property interest is material. Based on your facts, the City ofNovato's decisions  
about Hamilton Fields could have a material financial effect on your real property under two 
circumstances: a change in the character of the parcel, or an influence 011 its market value. 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 0)-(12).) 
Change of the parcel's character: We have found that a governmental decision will have a 
material financial effect on an official's property interests if it "[w]ould change the character of the 
parcel ofreal property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(IO), emphasis added.) To determine whether an

property, since you live in a flat, built-out neighborhood. 
Privacy, Noise & Air Quality: The City of Novato has not yet completed its EIR on 
Hamilton Fields. But the 1,650-foot distance between the proposed site and your residence 
suggests that there will be little, if any, effect on the privacy, noise or air quality of your property. 
!11.fluence 011 Market Value: Having established that Hamilton Fields would not change the 
character of your parcel, we must examine whether "the governmental decision [is] of such a nature

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you, a Novato City Councilmember, 
from participating in decisions about a proposed recreational sports complex approximately 1,650 
feet from your residence?

CONCLUSION: No. While the proposed sports complex could foreseeably affect your financial interest in 
your residence, any effect would probably be immaterial.
